54952.2.note: (auto cite-check, web) Confirmed via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov and secondary sources (FindLaw, Justia, Brown Act practice guides): § 54952.2(a) is the "meeting" definition, (b)(1) is the serial-communications/serial-meeting prohibition, (b)(2) is the staff-contact carve-out ("does not communicate... the comments or position of any other member"), and (c) lists exceptions — exactly matching the pipeline's usage in all three documents. Checked SB 707 (chaptered Oct. 3, 2025, eff. Jan. 1, 2026, current as of the July 2026 date in this session): it kept (a), (b)(1), (b)(2) lettering intact and only added a permanent (b)(3) for social media, so the subsection lettering cited is still current and the [delphi] doc's caution to "verify exact current subsection lettering" is satisfied rather than contradicted. Canonical: Cal. Gov't Code § 54952.2 (Ralph M. Brown Act) — subsec. (a) (definition of "meeting"), (b)(1) (serial-meeting/series-of-communications prohibition), (b)(2) (staff/employee informational-contact safe harbor), (c) (enumerated exceptions, e.g., conferences, social/ceremonial events). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=54952.2.&lawCode=GOV; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54952-2/; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2020/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54952-2/

54960.1.note: (auto cite-check, web) § 54960.1 exists and is the Brown Act provision letting a DA or interested person demand that a legislative body "cure or correct" specified action-taken violations before suing by mandamus/injunction to void the action. Subsection (c) sets the deadlines (90/30 days to demand, 30 days to cure, 15 days to sue) and (d) lists exceptions barring nullification (substantial compliance, debt/contract transactions, good-faith reliance, tax collection, adequate notice) — matching how tetlock and dialectic use the cite for deadlines, cure/no-response consequences, and scope. Notably § 54960.1(a) only covers violations of §§ 54953, 54954.2, 54954.5, 54954.6, 54956, or 54956.5 — it does not list § 54952.2 (serial meetings) — so dialectic's flagged question (whether 54960.1 textually reaches a bare 54952.2 serial-meeting violation, vs. general 54960) is a legitimate, well-founded scope concern rather than a misuse; likewise, the section contains no "knowing"/intent element (that language is unique to the criminal misdemeanor at §54959), so delphi's flagged open question is also accurate. No misuse found — the pipeline is correctly citing and appropriately hedging on genuine open scope/mental-state issues. Canonical: Cal. Gov't Code § 54960.1 (Ralph M. Brown Act, "cure or correct" demand and mandamus/injunction procedure). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54960.1.; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-1/; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2021/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54960-1/

54959.note: (auto cite-check, web) § 54959 exists and is the Brown Act's misdemeanor provision; confirmed via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov. Current text (as amended 1994) provides: "Each member of a legislative body who attends a meeting ... where action is taken in violation of any provision of this chapter, and where the member intends to deprive the public of information to which the member knows or has reason to know the public is entitled under this chapter, is guilty of a misdemeanor." This does establish criminal liability with a scienter element, consistent with delphi/tetlock's general use. However, the dialectic snippet's paraphrase — "with knowledge the action violates the Act" — is a simplified/older-style formulation that doesn't precisely match the current two-part standard (intent to deprive public of information + knowledge/reason-to-know of entitlement to it); this is a minor imprecision worth correcting if the mental-state element is being relied on with specificity. Canonical: Cal. Gov't Code § 54959 (Ralph M. Brown Act). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54959.

144 cal.app.4th 533.note: (auto cite-check, web) Confirmed via Justia and First Amendment Coalition (which independently cites it in the identical form) as a real, correctly cited 2006 First District Court of Appeal decision addressing Brown Act serial meetings and the "collective concurrence" standard for indirect/hub-and-spoke contacts among members — consistent with how [tetlock] and [dialectic] use it. The competing "(1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130" variant noted by one panelist in [delphi] could not be substantiated as this case and appears to be that panelist's error, not a defect in the 144 Cal.App.4th 533 cite. Canonical: Wolfe v. City of Fremont (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/2006/a112386.html; https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/asked-and-answered/collective-concurrences-serial-meeting-and-the-brown-act/; https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/2009/06/aa-serial-meetings/

54960.note: (auto cite-check, web) All three sections exist and match the pipeline's usage: § 54960 confers standing on "any interested person" to sue for injunctive/declaratory/mandamus relief; § 54960.1 sets out the cure-and-correct demand procedure (90-day demand, 30-day agency response, 15-day suit deadline) for specified violations; § 54960.5 provides discretionary fee-shifting to a prevailing plaintiff (and narrowly to a prevailing defendant if the suit was frivolous). Citation form and grouping are standard and accurate. Canonical: Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5 (Ralph M. Brown Act, Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54950 et seq.). Sources: https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-1/; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-5/; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54960-5/

18 cal.app.4th 781.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the cite (18 Cal.App.4th 781, 1993) is correct. But its holding is that an actual joint gathering of a board quorum (a Curriculum Council/Board work session where members together viewed a videotape) was a "meeting" because it involved "collective acquisition and exchange of facts" — it did not require verbal deliberation. The 'dialectic' and 'naive_sonnet' snippets instead cite Frazer for a "hub-and-spoke"/"daisy chain" serial-meeting proposition (no full-body gathering, information passed via a common conduit) — that is a different doctrine associated with cases like Stockton Newspapers, Inc. v. Redevelopment Agency and later Gov. Code § 54952.2(b), and available sources indicate Frazer itself distinguished its facts from that serial-meeting scenario rather than establishing it. So the citation is real but stretched to support a proposition the case doesn't stand for. Canonical: Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (1st Dist.). Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/4th/18/781.html; https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/asked-and-answered/collective-concurrences-serial-meeting-and-the-brown-act/; https://oag.ca.gov/system/files/media/the-brown-act.pdf

54952.6.note: (auto cite-check, web) Section exists and defines "action taken" under the Brown Act as a collective decision, a collective commitment/promise by a majority to decide, or an actual vote of the body upon a motion, proposal, resolution, order, or ordinance. All three document snippets use it correctly for this narrow definitional purpose, correctly tying it to the § 54960.1 cure-and-correct remedy which applies only to "action taken" in violation of enumerated sections. Canonical: Cal. Gov. Code § 54952.6. Sources: https://california.public.law/codes/ca_gov't_code_section_54952.6; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54952-6/; https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/handbook/california-brown-act-primer/

54960.5.note: (auto cite-check, web) Section exists and the cite form is correct. It authorizes a court to award costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a plaintiff who prevails in an action under §§ 54960, 54960.1, or 54960.2 by establishing a Brown Act violation, which supports all three snippets' use of it as the plaintiff-side fee-shifting hook. However, the "structural" snippet's flagged doubt about it being "truly one-way" is well-placed: the same section also lets a prevailing defendant recover fees/costs (in §54960/54960.1 actions) if the plaintiff's suit was "clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit" — so it's asymmetric (favoring plaintiffs, since the plaintiff standard is just prevailing/establishing a violation vs. the defendant's much harder frivolousness bar) rather than a strict one-way fee statute. Canonical: Cal. Gov. Code § 54960.5 (Brown Act; last amended by Stats. 2012, ch. 732). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54960.5; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-5/; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54960-5/

frazer v. dixon unified school dist.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case and reporter cite are real and accurate (3d DCA, 1993), but it is a textbook-selection/curriculum-review-committee due process dispute — it does not address the Brown Act, serial meetings, or the "hub and spoke"/"daisy chain" doctrine the documents cite it for. The pipeline appears to have attached a real citation to an invented holding. Canonical: Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/4th/18/781.html

147 cal.app.3d 518.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the cite (147 Cal.App.3d 518, 1983) is correct: it's a San Diego City Council Brown Act case where six of nine members signed a letter (circulated by staff) directing that summons not be served in an eminent-domain suit; the trial court found a Brown Act violation, that finding was unchallenged on appeal, and the Court of Appeal's actual holding is that the trial court abused its discretion in denying attorney's fees under Gov. Code §54960.5, reversing to award fees/costs. That supports the [delphi] snippet's tentative citation for standing/fee-shifting. But [dialectic]'s claim that the case "supports a broad reading of 'deliberate' ... the collective exchange and weighing of facts and views preliminary to a decision is itself deliberation" is not this case's holding — that formulation comes from Rowen v. Santa Clara Unified School Dist. (1981) 121 Cal.App.3d 231, 234 — and since the merits of the Brown Act violation were never before the Stirling appellate court, it announced no deliberation test. [tetlock]'s use as background legislative-history context for hub-and-spoke/daisy-chain concerns is a defensible characterization of the underlying facts, not a doctrinal overreach. Canonical: Common Cause v. Stirling (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518, 195 Cal.Rptr. 163. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/3d/147/518.html; https://caselaw.findlaw.com/court/ca-court-of-appeal/1837063.html

263 cal.app.2d 41.note: (auto cite-check, web) Citation is accurate (reporter, page, year, court all confirmed via Justia/FindLaw). The case does hold that "deliberation" connotes not just collective discussion but "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision" — exactly as used by [naive_fable] for the broad-deliberation point. The [tetlock] snippet's label of it as the "foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale" is a slight stretch on the facts (the case itself involved one physical luncheon gathering of the full board, not serial/successive contacts), but this broad-deliberation holding is routinely cited by Brown Act secondary sources (AG Brown Act guide, city-attorney CLE materials) as the doctrinal ancestor of the serial-meeting/rolling-quorum prohibition later codified at §54952.2, and the panelist appropriately flagged "verify continued vitality." Not a misattribution to the wrong case/section. Canonical: Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41 (3d Dist., decided June 12, 1968). Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/2d/263/41.html; https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1818917.html; https://oag.ca.gov/system/files/media/the-brown-act.pdf

5 cal.4th 363.note: (auto cite-check, web) Case exists and the cite form is correct (confirmed on Justia, Stanford SCOCAL, and other reporters). The naive_fable use is strongly verified: the opinion holds that an attorney's memo/letter distributed individually to board members is "passive receipt... of their mail" and not collective action, so no "meeting" occurs (pp. 374-376) — matching "attorney memo distributed to members individually was not a meeting." The dialectic use (liberal construction of open-meeting provisions, narrow construction of exceptions) is also supported: the opinion discusses the Act's "broad policy... to ensure that local governing bodies deliberate in public" (p. 374), and multiple secondary sources pinpoint 5 Cal.4th at 381 for the narrow-construction-of-exceptions holding — consistent with, though not verbatim identical to, the dialectic panelist's phrasing (who appropriately flagged the pinpoint page as needing verification). Canonical: Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363. Sources: https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/roberts-v-city-palmdale-31514; https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/4th/5/363.html; https://www.leagle.com/decision/citingcases/19933685cal4th3631356

54950.note: (auto cite-check, web) Confirmed via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov: § 54950 is the Brown Act's legislative findings/declaration of purpose, stating that public agencies exist to conduct the people's business openly, that "the people ... do not yield their sovereignty" to their agencies, and that they must remain informed to retain control over their government. This matches its use in all three documents as the liberal-construction/interpretive-backdrop provision favoring openness and broad construction against evasion — a well-established, textbook use of this section. Canonical: Cal. Gov't Code § 54950 (Ralph M. Brown Act). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54950; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54950.html; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2021/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54950/

sacramento newspaper guild v. sacramento.note: (auto cite-check, web) Case and cite confirmed exactly as given (3d DCA, 1968, 263 Cal.App.2d 41). It genuinely holds that "deliberation" under the Brown Act includes "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision," directly supporting naive_fable's quoted use. The [tetlock] characterization of it as the "foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale" is somewhat of a stretch — the case's facts involve a single gathering with an actual quorum present, not a serial chain; the explicit telephone-chain/rolling-quorum language is more precisely from Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363, 381, which built on this case's broad deliberation definition. Not enough to fail the citation, since it's flagged with a hedge in the source doc, but worth noting as an imprecise attribution rather than a false one. Canonical: Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/2d/263/41.html; https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1818917.html; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/roberts-v-city-palmdale-31514
